title: How I Stay Lean and Flexible Naturally at 48 (5 Simple Exercises)

chunks.title: Introduction to Fitness Philosophy
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces their fitness routine and philosophy centered around longevity, mobility, and fun rather than sheer strength or size. They emphasize the importance of working out for enjoyment and maintaining fitness as they age.
chunks.transcript: I'm 48 years old. I take no hormones, no drugs, and I'm pretty in shape. I'm going to show you in this video what I do in the gym. Most of the time when I'm in Costa Rica, I'm surfing and I do a little bit of gym stuff. When I'm traveling in the States, I do more. So, I'm going to take you through a full workout today. This is really how I work out to be [music] able to surf when I'm 90 years old. So, my goal is longevity. It's mobility, and it's strength at end range. And I'm doing this to maintain leanness and quickness. I'm not trying to be the biggest guy in the room. I'm not trying to be the strongest guy in the room. I'm trying to be the guy in the room having the most fun for as long as I can. I'm going to show you guys how I do it. I'm at Correct Fitness in [music] Austin. Shout out to my buddy

chunks.title: Mobility and Warm-Up Exercises
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses their warm-up routine focusing on mobility, utilizing bands for shoulder and hip stretches. They share specific exercises designed to alleviate the stiffness from sitting and improve overall flexibility and strength.
chunks.transcript: I'm going to start with mobility. I've been sitting all day. Been working, doing podcast, researching. Then I'm going to move into a little bit of strength stuff, but it's different than what you're thinking about. I'm going to think about knee strength, resilience, shoulders, all this kind of stuff. In the middle of the video, I'm going to show you a test that you can do for uh probably longevity or biological age. You got to wait for that one. It's going to be a test. We'll see if you guys can do this one. At the end of the video, I'm going to show you traditional strength training that I do, which is not what you're thinking. And then the cardio stuff that I like. One of the things I like about this gym, check this out. They have these garage doors here. And these are open. So, we're getting fresh air here in the gym, which is great. You guys might not know this. If you test the carbon dioxide levels in the gym, they're very high. That's a subject for a different video. But all the fresh air here is good. And the other thing I want to tell you guys right from the outset is I did not take any pre-workout today. If you need preworkout, you are not sleeping enough and you are under nourished. You are underslept and you are under nourished. The best pre-workout I think is just like honey and maybe some testicle pills. So that's what I use. I'm going to do like honey from Lineage and maybe some whole package. I don't think a pre-workout full of caffeine and things that are going to juice you up is is a good thing. Like if you need to push yourself that hard to get into the gym, just go take a nap and eat a steak, guys. So that's what I'm saying. Like you can easily overwork yourself, you can put yourself into adrenal fatigue. Working out is great. Exercise is great. It's a huge longevity lever. But if you need to push that hard to get into the gym, there are things to take care of first. I'm going to get some bands and start moving and get some mobility. So the first thing I do when I travel with this is I have a band like this, right? Just a rubber band. And you can use varying thicknesses. And I'll come in and I'll do these overhead kind of they're called shoulder dislocates. And you can vary the width of your grip based on your flexibility. I try and move the grip closer and closer because that's going to really stretch my shoulders. And you want to obviously keep a straight arm here. You don't want to bend your elbows. It feels really good, but a lot of people can't do that. So you may need to widen your grip. I'll do about 10 to 15 of those. Most of the stuff with with the working out, guys, I'm not I'm not doing a certain number of sets and reps. I'm just doing what I feel. So, then I come in and I'll do this and then I like doing deep squats to kind of like undo all the sitting. With these, I'm trying to get as deep as I can in the squat, flatfooted, and I'm trying to open my chest up as much as I can at the bottom of the squat. I'm not trying to get hunched over. So, this is really mobility. There's no weight here, but I'm getting a stretch in my shoulders. And then I'm transitioning it to my hips. Then sometimes you can even sit in this position and just breathe and just try and like fold your hips underneath. Go to posterior pelvic tilt. Open your shoulders up, look up as much as you can, and if you can't get to that position, just work toward it. You know, do as much as you can. The other thing I like to do here is I'll put this over my head, go to one side, trying to breathe into that side. So, I got there. Now I'm going to go deep squat. I'm just just getting mobile here, guys. One knee on the floor, another knee on the floor. Sit in that position. Then do the twist. If you can't deep squat like this and keep your back fairly straight, work on that. That's ankle mobility, hip mobility, thoracic mobility. This looks easy. It's way harder than you think.

chunks.title: Tests of Mobility and Physical Capability
chunks.summary: The speaker demonstrates a couple of tests that gauge mobility and physical capability, such as transitioning from a kneeling position to a squat and from sitting to standing without hands. They also challenge viewers to try these tests themselves.
chunks.transcript: if you can get from a kneeling position like this to a squat position, your biological age is less than 30. I don't believe it. Right? But you get the idea. So you can make it harder. That thing requires mobility. Right? You have to generate force from the hips. You have to mobility in your ankles. You have to go a deep squat. So test it. Can you go from this position to that? Let me know how you guys do. There's one more test that I've seen people do. I saw this in one of Will Tennyson's videos. He came out to Costa Rica to hang out with me. So, the idea is you go from a sitting position like this to a standing position with no hands. And it's doable. It's very hard. This one is definitely more advanced, but I think the way to do it is you go here. You have to be able to get to like a a pistol squat position. You move into the pistol squat position, stand up. So, it's another test for you guys. You have to be pretty mobile to be able to get your hips and your leg underneath you like that. I'll show it to you guys

chunks.title: Nutritional Philosophy
chunks.summary: The speaker shares details about their diet, emphasizing the importance of nutrition for performance and recovery. They describe the foods they consume, including raw animal products, and explain their belief in not training fasted.
chunks.transcript: all. I eat first thing in the morning when I get up. Then I ate maybe like an hour and a half before I went to the gym. This morning I got up for breakfast. chunks.title: Conclusion and Ongoing Improvements
chunks.summary: In the conclusion, the speaker reiterates the importance of fun in workouts and shows their enthusiasm for learning and improving skills in various physical activities. They express a desire to continue enhancing their form and abilities.
